Title: MAPK signaling – a key element in plant defense response to insects

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes systemic JA-Ile accumulation via electrical signaling.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Electrical signals generated via GLUTAMATE RECEPTOR-LIKE genes are sufficient to increase JA-Ile production in leaves. 2. These genes are implicated in systemic wound/herbivory signaling rather than direct biosynthesis. 3. They connect long-distance signaling with defense metabolite accumulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced prior work on systemic signaling and JA-Ile production
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned in perspective/systemic signaling context; not experimentally analyzed in this review itself.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes conversion of S-adenosyl-L-methionine to 1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylic acid (ACC).
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Stabilization by MPK6 increases ethylene production.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACS6 is directly phosphorylated by Arabidopsis MPK6. 2. Phosphorylation stabilizes ACS6. 3. Stabilized ACS6 increases ethylene biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Direct phosphorylation evidence cited from prior Arabidopsis study
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because explicitly linked to MAPK-mediated defense hormone accumulation in the review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes conversion of S-adenosyl-L-methionine to 1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylic acid (ACC).
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Stabilization by MPK6 increases ethylene production.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACS2 is directly phosphorylated by MPK6. 2. Phosphorylation stabilizes ACS2. 3. This contributes to elevated ethylene production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Direct phosphorylation evidence cited from prior Arabidopsis study

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 13-hydroperoxy-octadecatrienoic acid (13-OOH-18:3) to an unstable allene oxide leading to OPDA formation.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: allene oxide / immediate precursor of OPDA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely positively influenced by WIPK to promote JA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WIPK probably influences AOS activity in N. attenuata. 2. AOS converts 13-OOH-18:3 to the immediate precursor of OPDA. 3. This step contributes to herbivory-induced JA accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Inference from silencing studies and pathway analysis cited in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mechanistic regulation by WIPK is proposed, not directly demonstrated in this review.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: jasmonic acid; ethylene; salicylic acid; defensive metabolites
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: MAPK subfamily A, TEY motif serine/threonine kinase
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: phosphorylation signaling; positive regulation of defense hormone biosynthesis and defense gene activation
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: biosynthesis of jasmonic acid and ethylene; transcriptional activation of herbivore defense-related metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes herbivory-induced JA and ET accumulation and downstream defensive metabolite accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SIPK is rapidly activated by Manduca sexta oral secretions and by FACs in wounded N. attenuata leaves. 2. Silencing SIPK strongly reduces JA induced by simulated herbivory or wounding. 3. SIPK, but not WIPK, is involved in herbivory-induced ethylene production. 4. SIPK is required for normal accumulation of defense-related transcripts and metabolites and also contributes to systemic signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene silencing; simulated herbivory with oral secretions; FAC depletion/addition; MAPK activity assays; phytohormone measurements
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Wounding; Manduca sexta oral secretions; FACs
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: lipase releasing linolenic acid from chloroplast membranes; defense-related genes; ACS-like ethylene biosynthesis enzymes (by homology-based inference)
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates jasmonate and ethylene biosynthesis-associated processes and defense-related gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major positive regulator of herbivory-induced JA and ET bursts
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mechanical wounding; simulated herbivory; oral secretion elicitation; FAC treatment
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper; integrates evidence mainly from N. attenuata and homology to Arabidopsis MPK6.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: MAPK subfamily A, TEY motif serine/threonine kinase
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: phosphorylation signaling; positive regulation of jasmonate-associated defense metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: jasmonic acid biosynthesis and defense gene activation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes herbivory/wounding-induced JA accumulation and defense metabolite production.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WIPK transcripts and MAPK activity rapidly increase after wounding in tobacco. 2. Antisense WIPK expression reduces JA and defense-related genes in tobacco. 3. In N. attenuata, WIPK is activated by oral secretions and FACs and is required for full herbivory-induced JA accumulation. 4. WIPK likely regulates AOS activity and supports defensive metabolite accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Antisense suppression; gene silencing; MAPK activity assays; simulated herbivory; FAC depletion/addition; hormone and defense gene measurements
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important positive regulator of JA burst after herbivory/wounding
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Mechanical wounding; simulated herbivory; oral secretion elicitation; FAC treatment
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not involved in herbivory-induced ET production according to reviewed studies.

Regulation_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Nicotiana attenuata; Nicotiana tabacum; Arabidopsis thaliana; Glycine max
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: jasmonic acid; salicylic acid; defensive metabolites
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: species-dependent phosphorylation signaling; negative regulation of JA in N. attenuata; positive regulation of JA-responsive genes in tobacco
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: defense hormone signaling and JA-independent defense pathways
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: In N. attenuata, negatively affects Manduca sexta OS-induced JA and suppresses a novel JA-independent defense pathway; in tobacco, promotes JA-responsive gene expression after wounding.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MPK4 has plant species-specific functions. 2. In tobacco, wounding activates MPK4 and silencing compromises JA-responsive gene expression. 3. In N. attenuata, MPK4 does not regulate JA after wounding or Spodoptera littoralis OS, but negatively affects M. sexta OS-induced JA levels. 4. MPK4 suppresses a novel JA-independent defense pathway against M. sexta.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: VIGS/silencing; wounding and oral secretion treatments; JA measurements; defense assays with specialist and generalist herbivores
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Wounding; Manduca sexta oral secretions; Spodoptera littoralis oral secretions
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: JA-responsive genes; JA-independent defense pathway components
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of defense hormone output and herbivore resistance
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding; M. sexta OS; S. littoralis OS; drought; ABA
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: In Arabidopsis, mpk4 mutants show stunted growth and sterility
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functions differ strongly across species; review highlights distinct role in Nicotiana.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: jasmonic acid biosynthesis and JA biosynthetic gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes induced JA levels and insect resistance in tomato.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Manduca sexta feeding activates MPK1 and MPK2 in tomato. 2. Co-silencing MPK1/2 reduces induced JA levels and expression of JA biosynthetic genes. 3. Larvae grow better on MPK1/2-silenced plants, indicating reduced resistance.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression background; insect feeding assays; co-silencing; JA measurements; gene expression; larval performance assays
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Manduca sexta feeding; prosystemin overexpression background
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive regulator of induced JA accumulation in tomato defense

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Tomato homologue of WIPK / Arabidopsis MAPK
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: defense signaling associated with herbivory and aphid resistance
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activated by herbivory and associated with insect resistance, but direct metabolic effect is less clearly resolved than for SIPK/WIPK/MPK1/2.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MPK3 is activated in prosystemin-overexpressing tomato after Manduca sexta feeding. 2. Tomato MPK3, together with MPK1 and MPK2, confers resistance to aphids. 3. In Arabidopsis, MPK3 is activated by grasshopper oral secretions and wounding.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Feeding-induced activation assays; transgenic tomato studies; cited resistance studies
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Manduca sexta feeding; grasshopper oral secretions; wounding
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-associated regulator, but decisive metabolite control not fully established in this review
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because review highlights it as a recurrent MAPK in insect interactions, though nutrient-metabolite causality is less direct.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: upstream kinase activation of SIPK and WIPK
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for activation of SIPK and WIPK after wounding or simulated herbivory and influences trypsin proteinase inhibitor activity.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Constitutively active MEK2 strongly activates SIPK and WIPK in tobacco. 2. In N. attenuata, MEK2 is required for activation of SIPK and WIPK after wounding or simulated Manduca sexta herbivory. 3. MEK2 contributes to anti-herbivore defense output including trypsin proteinase inhibitor activity.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Constitutive activation; VIGS; MAPK activation assays; defense phenotype analysis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Immediate upstream activator of major herbivory-responsive MAPKs

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: upstream activation of MPK6 and MPK3
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: MAPK cascade linked to defense signaling
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activate MPK6 and MPK3 in Arabidopsis protoplasts, supporting defense hormone signaling cascades.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Arabidopsis MKK4 and MKK5 activate MPK6 and MPK3 when constitutively activated in protoplasts. 2. They represent upstream regulators of herbivory/pathogen-responsive MAPKs.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Constitutively activated kinase assay in protoplasts
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Upstream pathway component rather than direct product determinant
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as background MAPK cascade component in review.
